coredata.dc:title: Ga-doped carbon quantum dot-sensitized luminol molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor used for the efficient detection of cypermethrin in aquatic products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: You, Jinjie
Chen, Rui
Pan, Qiaofen
Zhang, Zeming
Sun, Aili
Zhang, Rongrong
Liu, Hua
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: The development of new electrochemical sensitizing materials is crucial for rapid detection technology. In this study, gallium-doped carbon quantum dots (Ga@CQDs) were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal approach to serve as coreactant for luminol. A molecular-imprinted electroluminescence sensor was prepared for rapid, sensitive cypermethrin (CYP) detection using in-situ electrodeposition. Ga doping reduces CQDs' band gap, boosts conductivity and catalysis, and enhances luminol's ECL signal. The advancement in molecular imprinting technology significantly enhances the sensor's ability to selectively detect CYP, while also providing robust interference resistance. The ECL intensity of the sensor correlated linearly with CYP concentration (0.05–100 μM) under optimal conditions (R2= 0.9912, LOD= 0.03 μM). In practical applications, recovery rates of water and aquatic product samples ranged from 86.4 % to 98.0 %, with RSD that did not exceed 8.13 %. A Ga@CQDs-based luminol sensitizing system for molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors provides a solution for fast, sensitive CYP detection in environmental and aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Molecularly imprinted polymers
CQDs
Cypermethrin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the Ga@CQDs and modified GCEs
Optimization of the MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE detection system
Selectivity, stability, and reproducibility of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE

This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32403026), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32303005), the \"Pioneer\" and \"Leading Goose\" R&D Program of Zhejiang (Grant No. 2023C02038).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CQDs and (B) Ga@CQDs, illustrated as corresponding HRTEM images; (C) Particle size and potential of CQDs and Ga@CQDs; (D) FTIR of CQDs and Ga@CQDs; (E-I) EDS mapping for Ga@CQDs; (J-K) MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE SEM images before and after elution template; (L-M) NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE SEM images before and after elution template.
Effects of (A) number of polymerizations turns and (B) ratio of template molecules to functional monomers and (C) concentration of luminol and (D) pH on the performance of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum, (B) EIS and (C) ECL curves of Ga@CQDs; Paramagnetic resonance spectra of Ga@CQDs (D) hydroxyl radical and (E) superoxide anion radical (F) Ga@CQDs sensitizing behavior on the electrode surface; (G-I) Comparison of ECL curves and ECL intensities of Ga@CQDs luminol and H2O2 luminol systems.

CV, ESI, and ECL characterization curves of different modified electrodes. (a, b and c are MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE after template elution, after template re-adsorption and before template elution, respectively; d, e and f are NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE after template elution, after template re-adsorption and before template elution, respectively).

(A) selectivity and (B) competitiveness of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE against cypermethrin and its structural analogs. (C) Selectivity and (D) competitiveness ECL curves of the MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. (Ccypermethrin =20 mmol/L and Cstructure analog =40 mmol/L).
Calibration curves of (A) MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE ECL response at different cypermethrin concentrations and (B) MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE for cypermethrin.
illustration of the fabrication and application of the MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE.

head.ce:title: Ga-doped carbon quantum dot-sensitized luminol molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor used for the efficient detection of cypermethrin in aquatic products

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new electrochemical sensitizing materials is crucial for rapid detection technology. In this study, gallium-doped carbon quantum dots (Ga@CQDs) were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal approach to serve as coreactant for luminol. A molecular-imprinted electroluminescence sensor was prepared for rapid, sensitive cypermethrin (CYP) detection using in-situ electrodeposition. Ga doping reduces CQDs' band gap, boosts conductivity and catalysis, and enhances luminol's ECL signal. The advancement in molecular imprinting technology significantly enhances the sensor's ability to selectively detect CYP, while also providing robust interference resistance. The ECL intensity of the sensor correlated linearly with CYP concentration (0.05–100 μM) under optimal conditions (R2= 0.9912, LOD= 0.03 μM). In practical applications, recovery rates of water and aquatic product samples ranged from 86.4 % to 98.0 %, with RSD that did not exceed 8.13 %. A Ga@CQDs-based luminol sensitizing system for molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors provides a solution for fast, sensitive CYP detection in environmental and aquatic products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cypermethrin (CYP) is a synthetic insecticide that mimics the structure of natural pyrethrin. It has broad spectrum, high efficiency, low toxicity and low residue characteristics and is widely used in agriculture, forestry, animal husbandry and household pest control [1,2]. However, pyrethroid residues are one of the most important pollution sources in agricultural production. They are frequently detected in water, soil, sediments, and coastal marine environments. Furthermore, due to its heavy use, CYP enters the nearshore marine environment through atmospheric circulation, surface runoff, and other effects, where it accumulates in aquatic organisms and increases the risk of exposure in the environment [3,4]. Related studies have demonstrated that CYP exhibits significant toxicity to aquatic organisms, with the potential to impair gonadal development, disrupt the secretion of sex hormones, and adversely affect the development of the nervous, immune, and reproductive systems[5]. Through the food chain, humans may be exposed to CYP via oral ingestion, which poses a potential risk of serious harm to the human immune system. Investigations conducted between 2012 and 2015 revealed that 16 % of respondents in Argentina reported the use of CYP in the production of fruits and vegetables. Consequently, numerous countries have established maximum residue limits for CYP, specifying that the permissible residue levels in various vegetables, meats, and cereal crops must not exceed 50 μg/kg[6,7].

The effect of CYP pollution on aquaculture and the safety of aquatic products are attracting attention worldwide, and it is urgent to strengthen the monitoring of CYP residues. Currently, the main detection methods for cypermethrin residues include gas chromatographymass spectrometry (GCMS) and high-performance liquid chromatograph–-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) [8,9]. However, these detection techniques require expensive instruments and professional analysts, require strict sample pretreatment requirements, and have disadvantages such as high cost and cumbersome operation steps, which prevent rapid detection. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a rapid detection method with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Molecular imprinting technology has become a popular quantitative chemical analysis method in recent years and is often used to prepare molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific recognition functions [10]. MIPs have not only good selectivity and specificity but also good mechanical stability, fast equilibrium kinetics and good reusability, which makes MIPs widely favored in analytical detection [11,12] . Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a process in which electrochemical intermediates undergo high-energy transfer reactions to form excited states and then return to the ground state to produce luminescence [13]. In recent years, ECL combined with MIPs (MIECL) has become a common detection method in biomarker detection and food safety. This analytical system has attracted considerable attention due to its high sensitivity, low background noise and strong spatial and temporal control [14,15] . The luminol–H2O2 system is a common combination ECL system, and H2O2 can significantly enhance the ECL efficiency of luminol [16]. However, despite the excellent ECL of luminol–H2O2 systems, H2O2 has great instability during the continuous ECL process, which limits its application in biological detection [17]. Therefore, the preparation of new coreactive agents or sensitizers is highly important for improving the ECL efficiency and application of luminol.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of good water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and good thermal/photostability and are widely used in biological imaging, photocatalysis, sensor detection and other fields [18,19] . Moreover, studies have shown that CQDs also have obvious quantum limitations and edge effects. The use of metal or nonmetal atom doping can effectively increase the surface trap density of CQDs, promote surface passivation, and enhance the electron transfer and optical properties of CQDs [20,21] . Moreover, CQDs can further oxidize the doped metal in ECL systems to yield certain enzyme-like properties and promote the formation of free radicals [22]. Gallium (Ga) is an excellent electronic raw material that is often used to make vacuum tubes, semiconductors and solar cells, and it can greatly improve the efficiency and stability of cells [23]. Therefore, gallium doping can not only improve the surface morphology of CQDs but also be used as an efficient electron transfer intermediate to improve the electron transfer efficiency on electrode surfaces, which has strong development potential. To the best of our knowledge, there are no relevant reports on the mechanism by which Ga-doped CQDs (Ga@CQDs) sensitize luminol ECL signals.

We propose a novel MIECL-Ga@CQDs platform with self-enhancing ECL signals for the ultrasensitive detection of cypermethrin ( Scheme 1). In this study, we anchored MIPs on the surface of Ga@CQDs-modified electrodes by in situ electrodeposition to amplify the ECL signal of luminol. Luminol was used as the luminescence signal, and MIECL-Ga@CQDs were used as the capture carrier to realize the specific recognition and quantification of cypermethrin in aqueous environments and living organisms through the ECL quenching effect. This method provides not only a new method for the sensitive determination of cypermethrin residues in the environment but also practical significance and a theoretical basis for the development and application of metal-doped CQDs.

CYP (> 98 %), bifenthrin (BIF, > 98 %), deltamethrin (DEL, > 98 %), fenvalerate (FEN, > 98 %), and cyfluthrin (CYF, > 98 %) were obtained from the Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute (Shanghai, China). White tea was purchased from Zhejiang Anji Songming White Tea Co., Ltd. (Anji, China). Urea, GaCl3, O-phenylene diamine (o-PD, > 98 %), and luminol (> 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and Tris-HCl were purchased from Beijing Solaibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). K3[Fe(CN)6] and Nafion (> 98 %) were obtained from SigmaAldrich (Germany). The remaining chemical reagents were analytically pure and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The white tea leaves soaked in pure water for 12 h were placed in a crucible, calcined at 200 ℃ for 2 h, removed, cooled, ground into powder, passed through 500-mesh sieves, and further calcined for 8 h to obtain the tea carbon powder. Then, 0.15 g of urea and 0.5 g of anhydrous gallium chloride were added, and 10 mL of deionized water was added. The pH was adjusted to 7, and the mixture was mixed evenly by sonication for 15 min. Then, the powder was calcined at 200 ℃ for 5 h in a muffle furnace. The products were centrifuged at 8.4 × 103 g for 15 min after cooling, and after centrifuging 2–3 times, the supernatant was pooled, lyophilized for 36 h, and redissolved in ultrapure water to obtain a Ga@CQDs solution.

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was ground to a mirror surface with a 0.05 μm Al2O3 slurry, followed by ultrasonic cleaning with ultrapure water, ethanol and ultrapure water 3–5 times to remove Al2O3 and impurities on the electrode surface, and dried with nitrogen at room temperature. The scanning potential was set at −1.0–1.0 V, and the scanning rate was 0.05 V/s. The polished electrode was quickly placed in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 for cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning until the curve stabilized. Then, the activated electrode was dried with nitrogen gas, and 15 μL of a mixed solution of Ga@CQDs and 0.5 % Nafion was dropped on the surface of the electrode. After drying in a nitrogen atmosphere, the electrode was placed in ultrapure water for CV scanning until the curve was stable, and the Ga@CQDs-modified electrode was obtained. Second, at a potential range of −1–0.5 V and a scanning speed of 0.05 V/s, Ga@CQDs-GCE was placed in phosphate buffer (PBS, 0.1 mol/L, pH 5.5) containing o-PD (100 mmol/L) and CYP (mmol/L). Scanning was performed for 15 cycles to obtain MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. Finally, MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was placed in a 30 mL methanol/acetic acid (7:3, v/v) mixture for 20 min with shaking to remove CYP. Moreover, the control blot Ga@CQDs-GCE (NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE) was prepared according to the same procedure used for MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE but without adding the template molecule CYP during polymerization.

ECL signal acquisition was carried out by an MPI-E electrochemiluminescence analysis system (Xi 'an Remax Electronic Technology, China). Other electrochemical parameters were collected by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments, China). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) was used to analyze the morphology of the modified electrodes, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy was used to analyze the Ga@CQDs morphology and lattice structure. The characteristic functional groups of Ga@CQDs and their chemical bond changes were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (FT/IR-4700, Jasco).

MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was detected by ECL in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 7.0) containing 10.0 mmol/L luminol. Then, 50.0 μL of cypermethrin was added to the electrolytic cell, and after 6.0 min of incubation, the ECL intensity at this time point was recorded as I. At this point, the photomultiplier voltage was 800 V, the potential range was −0.5–0.8 V, and the scan rate was 0.1 V/s. The ECL intensities before and after cypermethrin addition were recorded as I0 and I, respectively, and the ECL quenching efficiency of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was recorded as △I= I0-I.

The modified electrodes were electrochemically characterized by CV and EIS methods. MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was placed in a [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution (5.0 mmol/L) containing 0.1 mol/L KCl with CV scanning potentials ranging from −0.3–1.0 V at a scanning rate of 0.1 V/s. The voltage of the EIS instrument was set at 0.2 V, and the frequency ranged from 0.1 Hz to 1000 kHz.

The biological treatment method used was described by Pan et al. [24]. Briefly, 2.00 ± 0.01 g of shrimp meat (wet weight) was accurately weighed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube, followed by the sequential addition of 6.0 mL of acetonitrile and 0.6 g of NaCl. After vortexing and shaking for 5 min, the samples were centrifuged at 2.6 × 103 g for 5 min, extraction was repeated twice, and the supernatant was pooled and collected. Then, 5 mL of n-hexane was added to the acetonitrile extract to remove fat, and the mixture was shaken for 5 min and centrifuged at 2.6 × 103 G for 5 min to remove the upper n-hexane phase. The acetonitrile phase solution was collected in a test tube, dried and concentrated under nitrogen gas. Finally, the cells were redissolved in 2 mL of ethanol and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for subsequent experiments.

Fresh water samples were collected from the reservoir near Shrimp Wax village and filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane to remove suspended particles, the pH was adjusted to 7.5, and the samples were transferred to brown glass bottles for storage in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the Ga@CQDs and modified GCEs
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions can alter the original electronic structure of the carbon spot. As shown in Figs. S1A and 1B, Ag+, Cr3+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Ir3+ doped CQDs had basically no sensitization effect on luminol. The ECL signal of the Ga@CQDs-modified electrode was 8.9 times greater than that of the Mn2+ modified electrode. This is because the conduction band and valence band of Ga3+ are formed through sp hybrid orbitals, which have considerable band dispersion and increase the electron transfer efficiency [25]. Meanwhile, as illustrated in Fig. S1C, the addition of 0.5 mg of Ga at a pH of 7 resulted in Ga@CQDs exhibiting optimal sensitization of luminol. Furthermore, five batches of Ga@CQDs were synthesized under these optimal conditions (Fig. S1D), revealing consistent sensitization effects on luminol, thereby indicating the stability of the prepared Ga@CQDs. Moreover, Fig. S1E and F show that DPV and ECL signals of luminol are significantly enhanced in the presence of Ga@CQDs, indicating that Ga@CQDs is used as a sensitization agent in the system to enhance luminol's electroluminescence signal. To further verify the successful preparation of Ga@CQDs, the morphology of the CQDs before and after Ga doping was analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 A and 1B. The CQDs and Ga@CQDs are uniformly dispersed and have quasispherical structures. The illustration shows that the lattice spacings of the CQDs and Ga@CQDs are 0.21 nm and 0.28 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (100) crystal faces of graphene [26]. Fig. 1 C shows that after Ga doping, the particle size of the CQDs changes from 3.5 nm to 8.2 nm, and the surface potential changes from −31.8 mV to 10.8 mV, indicating that Ga doping causes an increase in the CQD particle size and an increase in the surface electropositivity. Fig. 1 D shows that the CQDs exhibit typical -NH, C-H and C-O stretching vibration peaks (3401 cm−1, 3110 cm−1 and 1294 cm−1, respectively), and there is a strong CN stretching vibration (1602 cm−1). There were strong C-O and CO stretching vibration peaks (1402 cm−1 and 1723 cm−1, respectively), which indicated that Ga doping caused further oxidation of the CQDs [27,28] . Furthermore, Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was performed on Ga@CQDs, and Fig. 1 E-I shows that Ga@CQDs contain major elements such as C, N, O, and Ga and have a uniform distribution of elements. These results indicate that Ga is successfully doped into the CQDs and causes lattice distortion of the CQDs. Subsequently, the surface morphology and structure of the modified GCE were observed and analyzed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 J, particles of uniform size and distribution were generated on the surface of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE, indicating successful formation of a molecular imprinting layer at the electrode. An imprinted pore cavity complementary to cypermethrin formed on the surface of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE after elution of the template molecules with elution solution (Fig. 1 K). As shown in Fig. 1 L and 1 M, the electrode surface was smoother than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE because the template molecule cypermethrin was not added during the preparation of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE, no imprinting sites were present, and there was no significant change in the electrode surface before or after elution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The binding and elution between the template molecules and the imprinted sites require a certain reaction time, and to obtain the best response, the elution and binding times were first optimized. Fig. S2 shows that ECL reaches a maximum and plateaus after continuous elution in a methanol/acetic acid (7:3, v/v) solution for 20 min, indicating that the cypermethrin in the imprinted membrane has eluted completely at this time, and a large number of imprinted cavities are generated for electron transfer. Moreover, when the eluted electrode was continuously scanned in 50 μmol/L cypermethrin solution, the ECL gradually decreased and reached stability after 6 min, indicating that the template and the imprinted cavity were complementary and that adsorption equilibrium between cypermethrin and the imprinted site was reached, which hindered electron transfer. Therefore, the elution and incubation times were 20 min and 6 min, respectively, in subsequent experiments.

Conventionally, the in-situ electrodeposition method is used to control the thickness of the imprinted film by controlling the number of electropolymerization cycles, and the number of polymerization cycles and the ratio of template molecules to functional monomers largely affect the imprinting effect of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE [29]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, as the number of polymerizations increased, the ECL quenching first increased and then decreased. When the number of polymerization cycles was 15, the ECL quenching value of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE reached a maximum. However, when the number of polymerization cycles is large, further thickening of the imprinted film occurs, which affects not only the elution and adsorption of cypermethrin but also the efficiency with which electrons reach the electrode surface. Conversely, the ECL of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was basically unaffected because NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE did not produce imprinted sites with specific recognition, so it had no adsorption or recognition ability for cypermethrin. Furthermore, Fig. 2 B shows that the ECL quenching of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE reaches its maximum when the molar ratio of cypermethrin to o-PD is 1:3. When the ratio exceeds 1:3, the ECL quenching value gradually decreases, which may be due to the excess o-PD covering the electrode surface during the polymerization process, increasing the rate of imprinted film thickening and resulting in difficulty in forming effective recognition sites on the electrode surface. No template molecule was added in the preparation process of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE which that lacks an imprinting site and selective adsorption capacity for cypermethrin, resulting in a significantly lower ECL quenching ability compared to MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. In summary, the optimal number of electropolymerization cycles and the template-to-monomer molar ratio were 15 and 1:3, respectively.

As the main luminescent substance, luminol produces a continuous and stable ECL signal, which is an important indicator for evaluating the sensor. As shown in Fig. 2 C, with increasing luminol addition, the ECL quenching value of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE first increased and then decreased, and the ECL quenching value reached a maximum when the concentration of luminol was 40 μmol/L. This occurred because with increasing luminol, a large amount of the excited-state intermediate material was produced in the electrolytic cell, increasing the ECL response intensity of the system and the quenching value. However, when the luminol concentration further increased, the excited-state material accumulated in the imprinted cavity, hindering the electron transfer efficiency and resulting in a decrease in the ability of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE to adsorb cypermethrin, and the ECL quenching decreased. The surface of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE did not create an imprinted cavity to provide an electron transfer channel, so the ECL quenching was much lower than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE and remained largely unchanged with luminol. Moreover, previous studies have shown that a strongly acidic or alkaline environment can affect the ion state in the imprinted cavity and weaken the formation of hydrogen bonds between the template molecules and the imprinted sites, which in turn affects the recognition ability of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 2 D, the ECL quenching of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE first increased and then decreased as the pH increased from 6.0 to 8.5 and reached a maximum at pH 7.5. This may be because H+ affected the ion state in the imprinted cavity and accelerated the dissociation of template molecules from the imprinted cavity under weakly acidic conditions [30]. However, when the pH further increased, cypermethrin decomposed in a weakly alkaline environment, and the ECL quenching decreased accordingly. Comparatively, NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE has no imprinted site and is therefore not affected by pH, and the ECL quenching value is basically unchanged and much lower than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. Therefore, a luminol concentration of 40 μmol/L and Tris-HCl buffer pH of 7.5 were chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Normally, the outer electron band gap of materials decreases as the lattice constant of the material increases. Previous studies have shown that the smaller the band gap is, the lower the energy required for electron transition, and the easier it is for the electron transition to occur, the better the fluorescence intensity and electrical conductivity of the material [31,32] . Fig. 1 A and 1B show that the lattice constant after Ga doping increases from 0.21 nm to 0.28 nm. Moreover, Fig. 3 A also shows a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity, indicating that the band gap of the CQDs decreased after Ga doping. Fig. 3 B and C show that the resistance of the Ga@CQDs-modified electrode is significantly reduced, and the ECL is significantly increased, indicating that the conductivity of Ga@CQDs increases with decreasing band gap, which is basically consistent with previous studies. Furthermore, the ECL intensity of the luminol-Ga@CQDs system significantly increased, indicating that Ga@CQDs can improve the electron transfer efficiency between luminol and the electrode surface and significantly amplify the ECL signal of luminol. Second, CQDs with smaller band gaps can promote the separation of electrons and holes and can catalyze the ECL reaction of luminol in the system [33]. Moreover, previous studies have confirmed that the ECL of luminol is closely related to the free radicals produced in the system [34]. As shown in Fig. 3 D and E, both the numbers of hydroxyl free radicals and superoxide anion free radicals generated by the electrolysis of Ga@CQDs in the system are greater than those generated by the electrolysis of CQDs, indicating that Ga@CQDs exhibit catalytic activity with electrification at the same concentration, which increases the rate and content of free radicals generated by dissolved oxygen and water in the solution. Therefore, Ga doping reduces the band gap of CQDs and improves the conductivity of Ga@CQDs. Moreover, its nano-enzyme properties accelerate the generation of free radicals under energized conditions, which is the fundamental cause of the improved ECL strength of luminol [17,34] . Based on the relevant experimental results, we proposed a specific reaction mechanism for the Ga@CQDs-mediated enhancement of the luminol ECL signal. As shown in Fig. 3 F, the excellent catalytic and electrical conductivity of Ga@CQDs promotes the reduction of some of the dissolved oxygen and water in the electrolyte to produce a large amount of •OH under energized conditions. •OH, reacts with the electron-losing anion (L-) in luminol to produce LO2 2-, and LO2 2- further reacts to produce the excited AP2-*. The release of energy produces a strong ECL signal. To further verify the sensitization effect of Ga@CQDs, the Ga@CQDs–luminol system was compared with the H2O2 luminol system at the same concentration, as shown in Fig. 3 G–I. The ECL intensity of the Ga@CQDs system was three times that of the H2O2 system. Moreover, the Ga@CQDs ECL system produces a very stable ECL signal (Fig. 3 G) due to the excellent electrical conductivity of Ga@CQDs, which is critical for rapid detection technologies.

Subsequently, the electrochemical properties of the prepared MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE were evaluated using CV curves. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when functional monomers and template molecules are electrodeposited on the surface of Ga@CQDs/GCE, the peak currents of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE and NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE decrease, indicating that dense nonconductive and hydrophobic MIP and NIP layers formed on the electrode surface. The peak current at MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE clearly changes after cypermethrin elution, which is due to the formation of imprinting holes favorable for electron transport at MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. Moreover, the peak current of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE is basically unchanged before and after elution because NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE does not contain an imprinting cavity, the electron transfer pathway is blocked, and the peak current is much lower than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE [35]. Subsequently, EIS and ECL were used to characterize and analyze the kinetics of the different modified electrodes (Fig. 4 B and C). When the MIP and NIP layers are electrodeposited on the electrode surface, the resistance increases, and the ECL decreases. This is because the tight structure and nonconductive nature of the MIP and NIP layers hinder the flow of electrons through the imprinting layer to the electrode, increasing the resistance and decreasing the ECL. After the template was removed, the resistance of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE decreased significantly, and the ECL increased significantly due to the formation of an imprinted cavity, which improved the electron conductivity. Conversely, the electron conductivity of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE before and after elution did not significantly change, the resistance was much greater than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE, and the ECL was much lower than that of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE, indicating that no imprinted cavity formed for electron transfer without the addition of template molecules.

Overall, Ga@CQDs with excellent electrochemical properties were successfully grafted onto the surface of the GCE, which improved the electrical conductivity during the luminol luminescence reaction and significantly enhanced the ECL efficiency of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability, and reproducibility of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE
ce:section.ce:para: CYP and its analogs, such as cyfluthrin, bifenthrin, deltamethrin and fenvalerate (Fig. S3), were selected as interfering substances to demonstrate the specific recognition ability of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. The selectivity of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was evaluated by calculating the △IECL of CYP structural analogs under optimal conditions, where the concentration of CYP was 20 mmol/L and the concentration of other structural analogs was 40 mmol/L. As shown in Fig. 5 A and Fig. 5 C, MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE had better △IECL and quenching efficiency than did its analogs. The △IECL of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was greater than that of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. In contrast, the △IECL values of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE for CYP and its analogs were basically the same, indicating that there is no specific recognition site on the surface of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. In addition, the anti-interference ability of the developed MIECL sensor was tested by competitive experiments. The △IECL of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE was determined by mixing CYP with structural analogs. As shown in Fig. 5 B and Fig. 5 D, there was no difference in the △IECL values of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE in the mixture of CYP and its analogs, indicating that MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE has excellent selectivity for CYP and negligible interference with its analogs. Moreover, the △IECL values of NIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE for CYP and its analogs did not change significantly due to the lack of recognition sites. Therefore, the constructed MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE has excellent selectivity and specificity for CYP.

Stability and reproducibility are other important properties for evaluating the performance of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE. As shown in Fig. S4A, after 10 consecutive scanning cycles, the ECL signal of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE remained basically stable, with an RSD of 4.59 %, indicating that MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE had good stability. Moreover, to verify the reproducibility of the constructed MIECL sensor, five separate MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCEs were prepared (Fig. S4B), and the five electrodes were adsorbed in 50 μmol/L CYP solution. The RSD of the quenching degree of the ECL signal for different electrodes was 7.61 % under the same detection conditions, indicating that the prepared method has good reproducibility. To further evaluate the reusability of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE, five consecutive elution and re-adsorption experiments were conducted (Fig. S4C). The results indicated that there was no significant change in the ECL signal of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE after five consecutive uses. However, the recognition capability of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE for CYP began to slightly decrease after the third elution cycle. This decline may be attributed to excessive elution and binding, which could compromise the integrity of the recognition sites on the blot layer.

To evaluate the practicality of the sensor, the linearity, recovery rate and relative standard deviation of the sensor were first evaluated. Under the optimal conditions, the ECL intensity of MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE decreased gradually with increasing CYP concentration ( Fig. 6). There was a good linear relationship between the ECL intensity and the logarithm of the CYP concentration in the range of 0.05–100 μmol/L; the linear equation was y = -360.39lgCCYP+ 917.08, R2 was 0.9912, RSD was less than 6.74 %, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.03 μmol/L (3σ/S, where σ/S is the signal-to-noise ratio, σ is the relative standard deviation of 10 blank samples, and S is the slope of the standard curve). The developed MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE sensor method was used to detect cypermethrin in aquatic products and seawater samples to further evaluate the feasibility of the method in practical applications. As shown in Table S1, the standard addition recoveries of the MIECL-Ga@CQDs-GCE sensor in actual water samples and aquatic products were 86.4–98.0 % and 88.1–95.6 %, respectively, with RSDs lower than 8.13 %, indicating that the method has good accuracy and can be used for the detection of actual samples. Furthermore, the developed MIECL sensor was compared with reported sensor methods, as shown in Table S2. The MIECL sensor prepared in this study is superior to other methods in terms of its linear range, accuracy and stability. Therefore, the MIECL sensor developed based on Ga@CQDs-sensitized luminol ECL signals has the advantages of simple sample processing, low cost and low solvent consumption and has good practicability for the determination of cypermethrin residues in seawater and aquatic products.

Herein, Ga@CQDs, which can enhance the ECL signal of luminol, were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method, and the mechanism by which Ga@CQDs enhance the ECL intensity was investigated for the first time. The band gap of CQDs can be well regulated, and their conductivity can be improved by doping Ga, thus accelerating the generation of free radicals in the electrochemical process and significantly enhancing the ECL signal of luminol. Moreover, a molecularly imprinted electroluminescence sensor with Ga@CQDs as a signal amplification carrier was prepared by in situ electrodeposition to achieve rapid determination of CYP in the environment. In the optimal detection system, the ECL intensity showed a good linear relationship with the CYP concentration (0.05–100 μmol/L), the detection limit was 0.03 μmol/L, the standard recovery was 86.4 %–98.0 %, and the RSD was < 8.13 %. The sensor prepared in this study is superior to traditional detection methods in terms of detection range and detection efficiency and has good application potential for environmental and aquatic product safety. Furthermore, this work provides not only a new perspective for elucidating the mechanism of electrochemical signal sensitization but also a new way to construct a rapid detection platform based on molecular imprinted electrochemiluminescence.

Qiaofen Pan: Validation, Data curation. Zeming Zhang: Validation, Supervision. Rui Chen: Validation, Data curation. Hua Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Xizhi Shi: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Aili Sun: Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Rongrong Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Jinjie You: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32403026), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LQ24C190002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32303005), the "Pioneer" and "Leading Goose" R&D Program of Zhejiang (Grant No. 2023C02038), Ningbo International Collaboration Project (Grant No. 2024H003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.137219.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinjie You received his Master’s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. He is currently studying for his PhD at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Rui Chen received his bachelor’s degree from Yibin University in 2021. He is currently studying for his Master’s degree at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Qiaofen Pan received her Master’s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. Her main research interests are the construction of electrochemical sensors and their rapid detection techniques for herbicide residues.

Zeming Zhang received his BS and PhD degree from Ocean University of China in 2013 and 2018. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the detection, migration and transformation of organicpollutants.

Aili Sun received her Dr’s degree from Ningbo University in 2017. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the development and application of fluorescence sensors for food safety.

Rongrong Zhang received her Dr’s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the detection, migration and transformation of organicpollutants.

Hua Liu received her Dr’s degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the applications of fluorescent probes and fluorescence sensing in environmental and biological diagnostics.

Xizhi Shi received his Dr’s degree from Shanghai Jiaotong University in 2008. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
